entry.title: Re: Unable to maintain reason for price change in Manage...
entry.content:
Dear @ijlsanthosh,

Option 1: Best if you need reporting

Use key user extensibility to add:

  • Reason for Price Change (Code list / dropdown)
  • Remarks (Text)

This is a standard and supported approach for Manage Prices Sales extensibility in Public Cloud

SAP Note beow:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3043473

High-level steps:

  1. Open Custom Fields
  2. Create fields in the relevant pricing business context
  3. Enable Usage for the Manage Prices Sales UI and Publish
  4. In Manage Prices Sales, use Adapt UI if the field is not automatically placed on the screen
  5. If you use spreadsheet upload, your custom fields should appear in the template after publishing also

IMPORTANT: Values disappear after Save. SAP has a known issue pattern where a custom field shows on the UI but doesn’t persist if required configuration steps are missed SAP points to the Extensibility for Manage Prices Sales setup steps for that

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3535580

Option 2: Approval Workflows for Prices in Sales

If your need is primarily why was the price changed with approval traceability the workflow route is usually the cleanest governance pattern

Critical prerequisite: the condition table used must be created with release status, otherwise Request Approval stays greyed out. In Public Cloud you do this via 103119 Create Condition Tables for Pricing in Sales in CBC

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3517093

Summary:

  • If your business wants the reason visible and reportable with the price use option 1
  • If your business wants controlled changes + traceability use option 2

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Depreciation Posted Monthly Despite Planned Useful Li...
entry.content:
Hi ,

In our case, the asset was originally created with a useful life of 5 years. Later, we identified that the correct useful life should have been 3 years. To correct this, I updated the useful life in the asset master data accordingly.

After making the change, I executed AFAB for the depreciation run. As expected, the monthly depreciation amounts were adjusted, and the system recalculated the full depreciation based on the updated 3-year useful life.

For reference, AFAR is used when we need to recalculate depreciation values after changes to parameters such as useful life, depreciation key logic, shifts, etc. It ensures that base values and dependent calculations are fully updated.

Thanks

Nag

entry.title: 🇧🇷 Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Joinville, Brazil)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core and Python. You do not need any background in the topic, but you should have a lot of curiosity!

In this CodeJam, you will learn how to use the Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). This approach improves the responses of large language models (LLMs) and reduces hallucinations. You will learn how to deploy an LLM and the orchestration service on SAP AI Core, and how to query it via SAP AI Launchpad and the Python SDK. Additionally, you will gain insights into key generative AI concepts and learn how to enhance RAG responses.

This is a free in-person event and is planned for Friday, March 13, 2026 in Joinville from 09:00 to 15:00. The language of the event is in Portuguese and the content is in English.

The event is part of the SIT Joinville that will take place the following day.

The instructors is @jessicademarchi!

Location: Ágora Tech Park, Rua Dona Francisca, 8300 - Distrito Industrial, Joinville

Please note that spaces are limited; we can only accommodate 30 attendees. If you wish to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. A few weeks before the event, we will ask you to confirm your attendance.

Prerequisites: Any Chromium-based web browser, such as Microsoft Edge, Google Chrome etc.

Visit the SAP CodeJam group for more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees.

entry.title: Re: Settlement Management - Condition Contract - system e...
entry.content:
Hi @Dan_Str ,

Yes, I'm aware of this scenario. As mentioned in other SAP Community threads, even when using proformas, you'll still need to work with a dedicated Settlement Document Type - which are not related to condition contract / single document - that doesn’t post to accounting.

To handle both billing data and proformas, you'll need to build a custom CDS view or structure that merges data from:

  • Standard SD billing (e.g. VBRK/VBRP)

  • Proforma sources (e.g. WBRK/WBRP)

Regardless of the direction, integrating proformas will require a custom CDS view or logic to aggregate and align both data sources for use in reporting or settlement.

You could also create proforma on SD side instead of using Settlement document type, but the standard CDS view VWB2_VBRKVBRP_1 will not capture them either hence new CDS view inevitable. 

Regards, 
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: How to automate NBP T-1 Exchange Rate logic in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am working on an S/4HANA Public Cloud project for a client in Poland. We have successfully automated the exchange rate feed from the National Bank of Poland (NBP) using the standard Datafeed (Refinitiv/MRM).

Polish VAT law requires using the exchange rate from the last working day preceding the service delivery (T-1 rule).

The automated feed correctly saves the rate with its true publication date (e.g., Jan 13 rate valid from Jan 13).

For an invoice created on Jan 14, the system should automatically pick up the Jan 13 rate.

The Issue: In standard SAP, the billing document looks for a rate matching the billing date. The customer currently uses a manual workaround by "shifting" the validity date of the Jan 13 rate to Jan 14 during manual upload so the system finds it. Since we are moving to automation, we can no longer "fake" the validity dates.

Question: What is the best practice in Public Cloud to handle this? I am considering using the BAdI SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER to set the Exchange Rate Date to Billing Date - 1.

- Is this the recommended approach for Poland local requirements?

- Are there any pitfalls with this BAdI approach regarding intercompany or credit memos?

- Is there a standard configuration setting I might be missing that handles this "look-back" logic natively?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Український бізнес у режимі змін: інсайти від керівної директорки SAP Ukraine
entry.content:
Дві розмови з Ганною Мікулицькою, керівною директоркою SAP Ukraine, про те, як зараз працює бізнес в Україні і про роль технологій.

Процеси та гігієна даних

У розмові з KPMG Ганна розповіла, як цифрова трансформація стала ключовою умовою стійкості українського бізнесу в поточних (чи теперішніх) надскладних умовах:

  • Цифрова трансформація сьогодні — базова умова стійкості бізнесу. Війна прискорила перегляд ІТ-інфраструктури та впровадження сучасних цифрових рішень, що підвищують конкурентоспроможність на глобальному рівні.
  • Технологічний сектор — це не лише IT-компанії: сьогодні технології присутні майже в кожній організації — від банків і телекомів до державних установ.
  • Штучний інтелект та дані. Щоб AI працював ефективно, потрібні структуровані, якісні дані — це базова інвестиція перед автоматизацією.
  • Людський капітал змінюється: зростає роль жінок і перекваліфікація спеціалістів, зокрема ветеранів, а освітні ініціативи й програми розвитку цифрових навичок стають ключовими для майбутнього екосистеми.
  • Відмова від ворожого софту — це не лише безпека, а й ефективність. Перехід на сучасні цифрові продукти може значно підвищити ефективність економіки та створити нові можливості для бізнесу.

Оборонка, кібербезпека та ментальне здоров’я

У випуску Let’s Talk разом із Європейською Бізнес Асоціацією Ганна Мікулицька, керівна директорка SAP Ukraine, поділилася баченням того, як український бізнес адаптується до нової реальності та які зміни посилюють його конкурентоспроможність.

  • Темп змін в Україні випереджає європейські ринки.
    Трансформації відбуваються швидко, і в цьому контексті технології стають не розкішшю, а стратегічною необхідністю.
  • Люди — ключовий ресурс.
    Ганна обговорює, яким чином внутрішня стійкість, підтримка команд та увага до ментального здоров’я формують більш гнучку й ефективну організаційну культуру.
  • Україна — інноваційний хаб.
    У відео йдеться про те, чому Україна має всі шанси стати майданчиком для тестування нових технологій і привертати міжнародний інтерес.
  • Кібербезпека та ШІ — про практичний вплив.
    Це не абстрактні тренди: кібергігієна стає частиною бізнес-культури, а штучний інтелект — інструментом для реальних рішень.
  • Лідерство без дистанції.
    Ганна також торкається теми сучасного лідерства — як воно змінюється та що означає бути в контексті молодших поколін

Переходьте до перегляду повних інтерв'ю за посиланням:

KPMG Review magazine

EBA, Let’s Talk

А після перегляду чекаємо на вас у коментарях. Що вас зачепило найбільше? З чим ви погоджуєтесь, а з чим — ні?

entry.title: Triggering Australian Compliance Forms for New Hires under a PNG Legal Entity (PNG Expats)
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am working on an Onboarding 2.0 requirement and need advice on triggering standard country-specific compliance forms when the Legal Entity's country does not match the candidate's required compliance forms.

Scenario: We are onboarding Expats into Papua New Guinea (PNG). The Legal Entity is Lihir Gold Limited (Country: PNG). However, the majority of these expats are from Australia (AUS) and the business requires them to complete the standard Australian Tax File Number Declaration and Superannuation Standard Choice forms within the system.

The Problem: Currently, the system defaults compliance requirements based on the Company/Legal Entity country. Since the Legal Entity is PNG, the system does not automatically trigger the Australian compliance tile or the "Standard" Australian forms.

Requirements:

  1. Conditional Trigger: We only want to trigger these Australian forms if the candidate is from Australia.

  2. Standard Forms: We prefer to use the standard SAP-delivered Australian compliance objects rather than building custom PDF forms if possible.

  3. No Global Mobility: We are using a direct-hire "Single Record" approach (no Home/Host record or Global Assignment is being triggered).

Technical Questions:

  • Has anyone successfully used a Field Metadata Override Configuration (FieldMetaOverrideConfiguration) to override the workcountry to "AUS" for specific legal entities/candidate types to force the compliance engine to trigger another country's forms?

Any insights on how to achieve this automation without resorting to manual form uploads would be greatly appreciated.

Environment: SuccessFactors Onboarding (2.0) Modules Involved: RCM, ONB, EC

Thanks in advance!
@Gopinath_Murugaiyan

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – How to configure automatic...
entry.content:
Good day @flavia_siqueira7f9753 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, this is in scope for Public Cloud.

You can handle this with the standard Output Management for the business document plus Brazil eDocument/NF-e process for legal document flow. It is advisable to use Output Management to send the PDF by email, while NF-e authorisation and monitoring stays in Brazil compliance apps. I will address your questions below:

  1. Question regarding setup using Output Management: For this, i suggest you use Output Parameter Determination to define the desired channel, recipient, sender, form template and timing rules for the relevant business application.
  2. Regarding which output types for DANFE PDF email, should you be addressing customer invoice scenarios, i suggest you configure the billing email channel, so the invoice PDF is automatically sent. For Brazil legal monitoring and document lifecycle, use the NF-e monitoring flow in DRC/Brazil apps.
  3. How to link Email Templates and Output Parameter Determination; I suggest you maintain the template in Maintain Email Template, then assign it in Output Parameter Determination in the email settings.
  4. Any best practice for automatic PDF sending after billing/NF posting. Focus on posting event and just keep your company code, sales org, output relevance strict. Make use of schedule output only for retries or backlog handling.
  5. Is this fully handled by Output Management in Public Cloud or does it require extensibility: Usually standard is enough for automatic PDF email. I would advice to only use extensibility if you need any custom logic, custom for fields or any advanced template that is not within the standard config.

Here is step-by-step guide as requested:

Open Maintain Email Templates app, copy a template for billing email, name it YY1_BILLING_EMAIL_BR, then set subject and body and save.

Open Output Parameter Determination, select billing business application. In the Email step, assign your YY1_BILLING_EMAIL_BR template, set your channel (Email. Receiver rule, sender rule.) and activate.

Open your BP customer and confirm email used. Open Maintain Business Partner or Manage Customer Master Data, then use the same BP, check address --> Communication --> Email. Ensure email exists, make sure it is marked as standard, the sales area partner usage is correct. Then run a billing test and verify email was received externally.

For Brazil legal flow, monitor lifecycle by opening Monitor NF-e app, filter by same commercial flow reference, confirm lifecycle.

In summary, the recommended approach is to:

  1. Maintain the email template using Maintain Email Templates app.
  2. Configure rules in Output parameter Determination for the billing business application.
  3. Maintain condition logic and activate it.
  4. Validate customer email master data.
  5. Post billing and verify output in monitoring apps.
  6. For Brazile legal flow, monitor NF-e lifecycle in the NF-e monitor app.

Basically, in most cases, you can manage this using the standard configuration. You only need extension for advanced custom behaviour.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP MDK Asset Manager random sync error 401 or host u...
entry.content: 401 error was caused by Cloud Connector and Principal Propagation. User certificate created by CC has 1 hour lifetime by default. Sync fails if certificates expire during the synchronization.

entry.title: SAP HANA and ABAP Performance Tuning: A Guide to Caches and Buffers
entry.content:
Background

It is a common misconception that S/4HANA is inherently faster simply because it runs on SAP HANA. In reality, achieving optimal performance for complex calculations and joins requires the use of tuning objects. These repository objects allow developers to define technical settings that optimize runtime behavior independently of the underlying data models.
In the S/4HANA 2025 environment, these objects minimize the "distance" between application logic and data in two ways:

  • ABAP Tuning Objects buffer records in the application server’s shared memory to eliminate unnecessary data transport.
    HANA Tuning Objects implement database-layer caches to significantly reduce CPU and thread utilization for intensive aggregations 
  • The core purpose of buffering is to minimize database load, hence increasing the overall system throughput and decreasing CPU consumption on SAP HANA database. Buffering avoids the high cost of repeated data transport between the Application Server (AS) and the Database. By keeping a local copy of data in the AS layer, the system can fulfill requests without ever reaching out to the network or the DB disk.

Performance tuning is not just about raw speed for a single user; it is about system-wide efficiency. By offloading repetitive row-level reads to the application layer, you free up the HANA database to focus on what it does best: complex calculations and massive data processing.

Sequence of Data Access

Apart from code pushdown introduced with CDS views, it is important to understand that when data is requested using CDS views, below is the sequence of steps:

  1. The Initial Request: System checks the local Entity Buffer. This is the ABAP Shared memory of the individual Application server instance. If data is not found then it is a cache miss and the ABAP SQL interface sends the query to SAP HANA database.
  2. The Storage Phase: From SAP HANA DB, the data is placed into Shared Memory of current Application server automatically by ABAP SQL Interface
  3. Subsequent Queries: For any requests henceforth, the ABAP SQL Engine acts as localized DB manager bypassing HANA DB entirely and reading data directly from ABAP Shared Memory

ABAP Tuning Objects

Our first choice of buffering should be entity buffering in ABAP Layer - it uses ABAP Shared Memory of the application server.

It is not enabled by default. The annotation  @AbapCatalog.entityBuffer.definitionAllowed: true should be mentioned in CDS entity. Below are different buffer types available in ABAP layer:

  1. View Entity Buffer: This buffer is meant for CDS View Entities and supports standard ABAP buffering types - Single, Generic and Full as well as DELEGATED 
  2. Table Entity Buffer: This buffer is meant for DDIC tables and was introduced in S/4HANA 2025 edition. Supports standard ABAP buffering types - Single, Generic and Full but DELEGATED is not supported
  3. Propagated Buffer: This buffer is meant for CD View Entities and uses path expressions to process joins in the buffer. Pre-requisite of this buffer type is the data source of propagated fields must be Fully Buffered. It allows ABAP SQL engine to process joins locally on application server by using data already available in the buffer hence preventing round trip to HANA DB. This can be very effective for a complex DB join.

Scenarios

  • When primary goal is to avoid repeated data transport between AS ABAP and DB Server
  • Data that is rarely updated such as localized text
  • Joins can be processed locally on application server itself  by utilizing data in the buffer

Implementation

Ensure annotation @AbapCatalog.entityBuffer.definitionAllowed: true exists in target CDS entity and define the buffer in ADT using below statements as applicable:

DEFINE VIEW / TABLE ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_entity
LAYER CORE
/ LOCALIZATION
/ INDUSTRY
/ PARTNER
/ CUSTOMER
TYPE SINGLE
/ {GENERIC NUMBER OF KEY ELEMENTS number}
/ FULL
/ DELEGATED| NONE
DEFINE VIEW / TABLE ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_entity
LAYER CORE
/ LOCALIZATION
/ INDUSTRY
/ PARTNER
/ CUSTOMER
TYPE SINGLE
/ {GENERIC NUMBER OF KEY ELEMENTS number}
/ FULL
/DELEGATED / NONE
PROPAGATE VIEW ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_entity
{
propagated_field1[,
propagated_field2][,
...]
}[;]

Monitoring & Maintenance

Buffering is automatically handled by ABAP SQL engine. Monitoring typically occurs through standard ABAP Shared Memory (SHMM).

HANA Tuning Objects

These objects are stored in memory of SAP HANA DB to save database resources and are oriented towards saving database resources such as memory and CPU consumption during heavy aggregations on massive tables. These are known as result cache used to improve performance by reusing previously queried data rather than reprocessing it from HANA Database everytime it is requested. They are defined in ABAP dictionary and implement technical settings directly on SAP HANA Database. Below are the types of result cache available in S/4HANA:

  1. Static Result Cache: This caches results for the specific CDS Entity View for a user defined retention period and is refreshed once that period expires. Best suited for complex views, KPIs, quarterly calculations where slightly stale data is acceptable to maximize performance. 
  2. Dynamic Result Cache: This caches aggregated results for single-table SQL views. Best suited for heavy aggregation workloads on massive tables where real time data is required. It automatically updates using delta records when source table changes ensuring most recent results.

Scenarios

  • Heavy Aggregation Workloads such as SUM, COUNT or AVG on massiv tables like ACDOCA or CDPOS (Dynamic Result Cache)
  • Analytical scenarios such as KPIs for complex views or quarterly calculations (Static Result Cache)

Implementation

SAP HANA tuning objects can be created from ADT using below syntax:

DEFINE STATIC CACHE cache_name
ON view_entity_name
{ element_list }
[RETENTION retention_in_minutes]
[SEGREGATE CACHE DATA BY filter_cond]
[CREATION CONFIGURABLE DEFAULT {ON|OFF}];
DEFINE DYNAMIC CACHE cache_name
ON dbtab
{ projection_list }
[WHERE filter_cond]
[SEGREGATE CACHE DATA BY filter_cond]
[CREATION CONFIGURABLE DEFAULT {ON|OFF}];

Use tcode S_DBCACHE_CONFIG to manually toggle the "DB Status" and activate the cache.

 Monitoring & Maintenence

Use DBACOCKPIT to monitor cache objects. The query M_DYNAMIC_RESULT_CACHE for real time hit rates and M_DYNAMIC_RESULT_CACHE_EXCLUSIONS to see views that failed to cache (e.g., due to 1GB size limits). For static caches use RESULT_CACHE. Use ALTER SYSTEM CLEAR DYNAMIC RESULT CACHE to globally remove all entries or ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE... for a specific cache ID. 

Conclusion

Leveraging both ABAP and HANA tuning objects in S/4HANA 2025 provides a robust framework for optimizing system performance while maintaining data integrity. The Dynamic Result Cache is essential for high-performance, real-time scenarios—such as aggregating the Universal Journal (ACDOCA) - because it guarantees transactionally consistent, non-stale results. By defining these as transportable ABAP Dictionary objects and managing them through tools like S_DBCACHE_CONFIG, you can significantly reduce CPU consumption and database response times across your landscape. While Dynamic Caches are ideal for real-time accuracy, Static Caches remain a strategic choice for complex views where periodic refreshes meet business needs.

entry.title: Festival Of People Analytics 2026: Celebrating the New Era of Workforce Intelligence this February
entry.content:
I had a chance to chat with Nigel Dias, managing director of 3n Strategy, and the chair and founder of the HR Analytics ThinkTank, to discuss this year’s Festival of People Analytics (FOPA) which is on Tuesday, February 24th 2026.  

All SAP SuccessFactors customers and SAP staff can attend for free, if they use the code SAP100 at the Checkout Page. To register to attend, please go here. 

Andrea Domhardt (SAP): Nigel, it’s great to speak with you again ahead of the Festival of People Analytics 2026. What’s the inspiration behind this year’s theme, “A New Era of Workforce Intelligence”? 

Nigel Dias (3n Strategy): We chose this theme because 2026 feels like a turning point for HR and People Analytics. With advances in AI, Machine Learning, and the arrival of People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud, organisations can move beyond reporting into predictive and more action-oriented analytics. This is a genuinely exciting time for anyone who is involved in improving people outcomes at their business - and we want to not just acknowledge that, but ‘celebrate’ it too!  

Andrea: Yes, it is exciting. For customers who are still early in their analytics journey, how should they think about this “new era”? 

Nigel: It’s normal to feel overwhelmed by all the new technology buzz, but the core idea is simple. Be clear on the HR questions you want data to answer, understand where the relevant data lives (whether in SAP SuccessFactors or in other non-SAP sources), and then use the right tools to bring it together and answer those questions. Tools like People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud can accelerate that process, but the mindset shift is what really defines this new era. 

Andrea: You’re speaking at this year’s Festival - tell us about your session on “The State of People Analytics in 2026.” What will you cover? 

Nigel: That session looks at the journey SAP SuccessFactors customers have taken with analytics - where they started, where they are now, and where they really can go next. It builds on more than ten years of polling the SAP SuccessFactors community and benchmarking how customers approach people analytics in practice. I’ll also share insights from our current SAP SuccessFactors Analytics Priorities Study, which looks ahead at where organisations are planning to invest, how ready they feel, and where analytics is having the greatest impact in 2026. The Festival will also feature People Intelligence demo, Q&A sessions with SAP experts, and panel discussions providing realistic examples, not just theory. That’s valuable for organisations trying to prioritise what to adopt first.  

Andrea: You’re also speaking about moving from SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Analytics (WFA) to People Intelligence. What should attendees expect from that discussion? 

Nigel: I’m speaking with a lot of WFA users right now and have used their most common questions to create this session. We’ll unpack the differences between Workforce Analytics and People Intelligence, with a clear focus on helping existing WFA customers understand the benefits of People Intelligence and how it supports more strategic people analytics. The emphasis is on preparation - what teams can do now to get ready for a move to People Intelligence in a way that’s realistic, manageable, and aligned to their business priorities. 

Andrea: Lastly, why should SAP customers make time for the Festival this year? 

Nigel: Because this is one of the few events where customers can hear directly from SAP, from practitioners, and from peers about how people analytics and intelligence really works in organisations like theirs. Whether you’re just getting started on your analytics journey or ready to scale it, you’ll walk away with ideas you can apply straight away. 
 
All SAP SuccessFactors customers and SAP staff can attend for free, if they use the code SAP100 at the Checkout Page. To register to attend, please go here.

entry.title: Re: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Upgrade Impact Analysis
entry.content:
Hi @aditya_palekar When upgrading from SAP S/4HANA 2020 to 2023, standard Fiori apps like F101 will not automatically transition seamlessly. The upgrade impact depends on the app's lifecycle status and any extensions you've implemented.

Key Recommendations

  1. Prepare Early: Activate SAP Fiori administration tools using task list SAP_FIORI_FOUNDATION_S4 before upgrade
  2. Document Extensions: Thoroughly document current extensions and customizations before upgrade
  3. Plan Testing: Successor apps may have different features, buttons, and navigation points requiring comprehensive testing 
  4. Leverage Improvements: Often successor apps provide improved extension options that should be part of your evaluation

The transition will not be completely seamless, but with proper planning and the systematic approach outlined above, you can ensure minimal disruption while taking advantage of the enhanced capabilities in the newer app versions.

Regards,

Bharanidharan S P M

entry.title: Achieve real-world results with SAP Business AI!
entry.content:
SAP Business AIGet ready! I'm kicking off a blog series in the coming weeks dedicated to SAP Business AI. We'll explore the 'what,' 'why,' and 'how' of this exciting technology.

SAP Business AI: Demystifying the Hype – What It Really Is

Ever hear the buzz about "SAP Business AI" and wonder what it means for your daily work? It's easy to get lost in the marketing jargon, so let's break it down in plain English. It's not about replacing everyone with robots (phew!), but about making your existing SAP systems smarter.

So, What's the Core Idea?

Basically, SAP Business AI is about adding smarts to your existing SAP setup. Instead of being a separate product, think of it like a layer of intelligence built into the SAP applications you're already using. It's about leveraging the power of AI to help you make better decisions, automate tasks, and generally get more out of your SAP investment.

Think of it like this: Imagine a regular car, now compare with self-driving car. The technology helping you to drive the car by itself. This is what SAP doing inside their product.

It pulls in different AI technologies, including:

  • Machine Learning (ML): ML helps SAP learn from tons of data, spot patterns, and predict what might happen next. This helps you make informed decisions.
  • Natural Language Processing (NLP): NLP is what lets you talk to computers using regular language. Chatbots and voice assistants are example.
  • Robotic Process Automation (RPA): We all have those boring, repetitive tasks, right? RPA automates those things, freeing you up for more interesting work.
  • Generative AI: You're probably hearing a lot about this! It's about creating new content – text, images, code – based on what it's learned. In SAP, this could help generate reports or create personalized content.

How Does This Actually Work? Real Examples

Forget the abstract concepts, let's get practical. SAP Business AI isn't a magic wand, but it can have tangible impact.

  • Finance: Imagine your SAP system flagging unusual transactions before they become a problem. AI can analyze financial data to find anomalies, forecast cash flow, and even automate invoice processing.
  • Supply Chain: Picture your system warning you about a potential disruption due to a storm in a key region. AI analyzes data to identify risks and suggest backup plans.
  • HR: AI can help sift through resumes, identify promising candidates, and even create personalized training programs. It can also analyze employee feedback to get a pulse on morale.
  • Sales & Marketing: AI can personalize customer experiences by analyzing purchase history, predicting needs, and tailoring offers. It can also automate marketing campaigns and optimize pricing.

Okay, But What's in It for Me?

Here's the thing: SAP Business AI isn't just about fancy tech. It's about making your job easier and your business more successful. It offers promise:

  • Less Tedious Work: Automating repetitive tasks means you can focus on what really matters.
  • Better Insights: Deeper analysis of your data helps you make smarter decisions, not just gut feelings.
  • Happier Customers: Personalized experiences lead to more loyal customers.
  • Cost Savings: Optimizing processes and reducing errors save money.
  • More Adaptability: Being able to respond quickly to market changes keeps you ahead of the curve.

The Takeaway: What You Need to Know

SAP is pushing Business AI, and it's becoming a bigger part of their offerings. While the marketing might sound a bit overwhelming, the core idea is solid: using AI to make your SAP systems more intelligent and efficient.

Instead of fearing AI, think of it as a tool that can help you do your job better. Explore what SAP Business AI offers in your specific area and see how it can benefit your team and your business. It's about making smarter choices and building a more resilient future, one intelligent decision at a time.

entry.title: Introducing the New SAP Activate page on sap.com
entry.content:
We're excited to announce the launch of the new SAP Activate page on sap.com, redesigned to clearly demonstrate how SAP Activate helps organizations successfully adopt and run SAP solutions in the cloud. Now live, the updated page makes it easier for customers, partners, and project teams to see how SAP Activate accelerates value and reduces risk throughout their transformation journey. 

SAP Activate combines SAP best practices, guided implementation content, and agile project delivery into a single, cohesive framework that supports organizations from initial discovery through deployment and into continuous innovation. 

This new page highlights how SAP Activate enables teams to get started quickly using ready-to-run business processes, guided roadmaps, and proven accelerators. It also reinforces the role of SAP Activate across the full solution lifecycle - from defining scope and preparing teams, to configuring and testing solutions, to driving adoption and long-term success after go-live. 

Visitors can also find examples of how customers are using SAP Activate to achieve measurable results, demonstrating how the methodology supports faster implementations, smoother transitions to the cloud, and a strong foundation for ongoing innovation.

In addition, the page connects users to key resources, such as SAP Cloud ALM, digital discovery assessment, learning journeys, and community content that help teams build skills and stay aligned throughout their projects.

We encourage you to explore the new SAP Activate page and see how it supports successful cloud adoption from start to finish. 

Visit the new SAP Activate page here.

Visit SAP Activate road map viewer here.

entry.title: AI Readiness: You don’t need an orchestra yet, you need a steady bass line
entry.content:
Any other big music fans in the procurement world out there? I know I am. And while I like music from most genres, what I really love is some intricate symphonic soul, like Isaac Hayes, or Curtis Mayfield. Strings, horns, keys, even a vibraphone. I get caught up in all the different sounds, the complex chords, how it all comes together.  But a real groove needs a great bass line. If that one thing is off, the whole production feels wrong.

That’s how a lot of my customer conversations on AI in procurement feel lately. Everyone’s focusing on those extras – AI assistants, instant answers and insights, getting to talk to AI like a person, and shiny demos of the vast possibilities with SAP Joule. And while those things DO matter, and they are the end goal, without that steady bassline carrying us through, those other sounds won’t land the way they’re supposed to.


So what’s the bass line in SAP AI?

In SAP landscapes, a solid foundation comes down to a few important things.

  • Clean, consistent identity and access (IAS)
  • Reliable integrations and flows of data (BTP)
  • And, incredibly important, clear ownership and usable data in systems like Ariba

When identity is fragmented, data between systems becomes inconsistent, or processes start to vary depending on who’s involved and who’s taking action. When this happens, AI doesn’t become on-beat and insightful, it becomes…improvisational, like jazz (but not in the good way, more the kind that just feels like chaos). We need to feel the rhythm first, since procurement is often where we see issues show up first.

Maybe your suppliers don’t line up across systems. Or risk is managed in a separate system, and doesn’t connect to anything today. And, likely the most common thing we hear, users work around the process because it’s faster than doing it the right way.

These things aren’t always a technology problem, but more of a rhythm problem.  Which is why building the foundation (bass line) is so important.

How are others laying down the groove and actually making progress? A few ways:

  1. Standardizing across their SAP applications:  This doesn’t mean everything has to be identical or locked down overnight. It usually starts much more simply: agreeing on how users authenticate, how suppliers are identified, and which system is the source of truth for what. When different SAP applications each have their own rules, logins, and data assumptions, things get out of sync fast. Standardization is what keeps the rhythm steady across the landscape instead of constantly drifting off-beat.

  2. Building strong integrations through BTP:  BTP is often talked about in very abstract terms, but in practice, this is about making sure data can move cleanly and predictably between systems. For procurement, this mostly means having supplier data flow consistently between Ariba and your ERP, risk info actually connecting back to suppliers, and integrations that are monitored and understood. When those integrations are brittle or one-off, that means AI is going to make guesses. We don’t want that.

  3. Cleaning up supplier lifecycles and ownership in Ariba:  Who owns onboarding? Who makes updates to supplier data? If you’re thinking, “It depends,” then that’s where problems likely start. Clarifying ownership of supplier lifecycles in Ariba doesn’t just improve data quality, rather it makes downstream automation and AI insights far more reliable.

  4. Making processes easy enough that users can follow them:  If a process is technically correct but painful in practice, users will work around it. Simplifying steps, reducing exceptions, and designing for how people actually work is part of the composition.

  5. Policies in place that are actually enforced, both internally and with suppliers:  Policies only help if they show up in the systems people use every day. When rules are enforced inconsistently (or not at all), data quality erodes, as does trust in the system.

That’s it, that’s the groove. Once you find this rhythm, the rest of the instruments can shine, take solos, and please a crowd. With that foundation, AI features become easier to adopt, and give better insights, and automation helps to reduce work, improve cycle time, ensure compliance, and other things you care about, instead of just confusing the people who actually have to use it.

entry.title: Document Migration: Understanding User Difference Between Onboarding 1.0 and BizX
entry.content:
It's important to recognize the distinction between two different types of users within SAP SuccessFactors: Onboarding 1.0 users and BizX users.

  • Onboarding 1.0 Users: These users are managed through the Onboarding 1.0 Dashboard under Security > Users.
  • BizX Users: These are the regular users who log into the SuccessFactors BizX platform. The user information is stored in the Universal Data File (UDF) and can be viewed in their Employee Central (EC) profile.

When you initiate onboarding for an individual, Onboarding 1.0 creates a user entry in its dashboard. However, this user is initially not present in the BizX environment. Depending on system configuration, the user may sync with BizX later, adding them to the BizX user list. Alternatively, you can directly create a BizX user in EC, which won't appear in Onboarding 1.0 unless a user sync is performed.

User sync is a configuration task designed to update or create users in Onboarding 1.0; it's a one-way synchronization, sending data only to Onboarding 1.0, not the other way around. Recognizing the existence of these two distinct user types helps illuminate the synchronization process. 

Consider a scenario where you have a user named "Jack" with different usernames between the systems:

  • Example 1:
    • User in Onboarding 1.0: Login "Jack123," first name Jack, last name Bauer.
    • User in BizX: Username "Jack," first name Jack, last name Bauer.
      • These two are completely different users, which means searches will not work, and documents will have "admin" listed as the owner.
  • Example 2:
    • User in Onboarding 1.0: Login "Jack," first name Jack3, last name Bauer3.
    • User in BizX: Username "Jack," first name Jack, last name Bauer.
      • These two are the same user. The name differences illustrate that the identifiers "login" and "username" are what matter. Searches will work, and documents will have "Jack" listed as the owner.

This distinction is crucial for understanding file management within the system. For files to be searchable under Manage Documents by name, the "login" from Onboarding 1.0 and the "username" from BizX must match; otherwise, the file owner may not be identifiable, hindering file searches.

entry.title: Re: Guidance Required: Technical Learning Roadmap for SAP...
entry.content:
Hi colleague,
SAP recommends SAP Build Apps as the main approach for extensions in Version 2 (as shown in this official SAP learning journey:
https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/extending-sap-sales-cloud-and-sap-service-cloud-version-2-using-sap-build-apps ).

However, Build Apps is designed for UI extensions and application-level logic, and when the development requires complex business logic, custom persistence, or reusable backend services, the technical recommendation is to implement that part using CAP on SAP BTP.

You can find the technology comparison in this SAP learning journey:
https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/extending-sap-sales-cloud-and-sap-service-cloud-version-2-using-sap-build-apps/identifying-key-features-and-limitations-of-extending-sap-sales-and-service-cloud-version-2-using-side-by-side-extensibility

Best regards.
Fernando DJ Espitia

entry.title: Re: Stock transfer of Quality and Blocked stocks with EWM
entry.content:
Hello, In my client system, there is one company code with two plants:

Plant 1 – Production plant (IM managed)

Plant 2 – EWM Warehouse plant

Both plants have different profit centers.

When GR is done for a production order in Plant 1, an inspection lot is created. Due to space limitations in the production plant, the material needs to be physically transferred to the warehouse (Plant 2), which is located about 5 km away.

However, the warehouse user does not perform GR until the Usage Decision (UD) is completed.

There is also another storage location in Plant 1. For example:200 quantity is available in SLoc 1

UD is completed for only 100 quantity

In this case:

100 quantity is moved to the warehouse through a stock transfer / sales purchase movement

The remaining 100 quantity is already physically moved out of Plant 1, but the UD is still pending

How can this scenario be handled properly in SAP? Any suggestions?

entry.title: SAP Developer News, February 5th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

SAP Build Q4 2025 Release Highlights

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/tooling-sap-build-blog-posts/what-s-new-in-sap-build-q4-2025-release-highlights/bc-p/14316927

My Home in SAP BTP ABAP Environment

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/my-home-in-sap-btp-abap-environment/ba-p/14314918

 

Did You Know? #2 – Mocking Auth in CAP

Devtoberfest 2025 – Winners Announcement

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2025-winners-announcement/ba-p/14320779

New live stream series and blog post series on CAP topics

  • Modules, modularity & reuse in CDS models (blog post series) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/01/01/modules-modularity-and-reuse-in-cds-models/
  • Hands-on SAP Dev CXL live stream series playlist https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQCEU8XcyqnA5wYEZGxMPm6B
  • A new Hands-on SAP Dev mini-series on the core expression language in CDS (blog post) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2025/12/09/a-new-hands-on-sap-dev-mini-series-on-the-core-expression-language-in-cds/
  • Constraints, expressions and axioms in action (blog post) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/01/27/constraints-expressions-and-axioms-in-action/

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 SAP Build Q4 2025 Release Highlights

1:36 My Home in SAP BTP ABAP Environment

3:04 DYK #2 Mocking Auth in CAP

4:10 Devtoberfest 2025 – Winners Announcement

4:44 New live stream series and blog post series on CAP topics

Transcription

00;00;00;29 - 00;00;05;28
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for February 5th, 2026.

00;00;06;00 - 00;00;09;25
Shrini Neelamegam
Q4 of 2025 was a huge season for SAP Build,

00;00;09;27 - 00;00;40;11
Shrini Neelamegam
and we have got some powerful new features to talk about. First up, the agent builder in Joule Studio is officially GA. With MCP server integration and the ability to link multiple agents together. You can now build production grade AI agents that actually moves the needle for your business. Plus, Joule is now baked into every edition of Work Zone, bringing context aware answers and proactive insights right to your team's fingertips.

00;00;40;13 - 00;01;07;17
Shrini Neelamegam
For the developers out there, you can now spin up Freestyle UI5 apps in SAP Build Code with a simple slash command. And if you are looking to automate, you have added new ways to trigger processes and build process automation, making it easier to bridge the gap between SAP and non SAP systems. On the design front end SAP mobile start just got a makeover with Apple's liquid glass look.

00;01;07;20 - 00;01;35;14
Shrini Neelamegam
And for the admins, Workzone sites can now handle thousands of documents and translations. We are just scratching the surface here. There is plenty more on decision tables, decision rules, HTML templates for notifications, and new CAPannotations. Head over to Frederick's latest blog post for full Q4 2025 release announcement.

00;01;35;17 - 00;02;07;13
Sheena M K
SAP has rolled out My Home, the new personalized, light entry page for the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. By default, from at least 2602, and it will be the one and only entry page from release 2608. My home is designed to boost productivity with a modular, customizable layout that includes key sections such as news pages, add and insights, and cards, all aimed at giving users quick access to the tools and information they interact with the most.

00;02;07;15 - 00;02;40;00
Sheena M K
While the new section keeps you updated with the latest company or system updates, beta section offers quick access to your most used or favorite pages. The apps section improves discoverability with favorite, recent and most used apps. Plus, AI powered recommendations based on role assignments via SAP Business AI. The inside 30s provide immediate visibility into KPIs and analytics and data, offering quick access to apps for further details.

00;02;40;03 - 00;03;06;08
Sheena M K
Following the “Make It Mine” approach, users can hide, reorder and restructure sections. The skew then working side across all devices. So start exploring my home for a clean, organized and smarter navigation for the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. And for details, check out the blog post from Laura in the description below.

00;03;06;10 - 00;03;33;19
DJ Adams
Did you know that CAP has a mocked authentication strategy you can use in local development? So you don't have to put off this important part of your domain design? Here's a simple service annotated with some auth requirements. Authentication is required generally, and there are some role based restrictions on one of the entities. With CDS watch we get this strategy out of the box, as we can see, along with some users and roles too.

00;03;33;21 - 00;04;01;05
DJ Adams
Here's Alice with the admin role. For example. Let's explore. How about trying to read the Book Stocks Entity? 401! We need to authenticate, of course. Let's authenticate as Alice. Now we have read access. What about write? 403. Nope. Alice doesn't have the requisite role. Let's fix that with some extra config in dot cdsrc dot Json, for example, which assigns her the back office role that's required.

00;04;01;08 - 00;04;09;21
DJ Adams
Okay, let's try again. Success output request as Alice is now accepted. Nice.

00;04;09;23 - 00;04;31;17
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
Hey, SAP Community, it's finally time! We have the full list of the ten Devtoberfest 2025 winners ready and online. You can find a link to the blog post down in the description of this video, so you can head over to the SAP community and see if you are one of the approved winners of last year's Devtoberfest.

00;04;31;20 - 00;04;42;27
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
And if you're one of the winners, you can look forward to a great fully paid trip to SAP TechEd this year. I can't wait to see you there!

00;04;43;00 - 00;05;21;13
DJ Adams
The developer advocates mission is to help developers. That's you be successful. To that end, we create lots of content in various forms CodeJam, tutorials, videos, talks, and lots more. We just wanted to highlight a couple of recent series in the CAP area. First, there's a blog post series on the topic of CDS modeling. The series is called Modules Modularity and Reuse in CDS models, and it explores how we can use modules for reuse in and across our projects and beyond.

00;05;21;15 - 00;05;53;15
DJ Adams
It illustrates this by first creating and publishing a simple passive reuse module before moving on to understand, explore and dig into the active Capire common reuse module. We're also running a series right now on our hands on SAP Devs live stream show. It's on CAP’s Expression Language, C XL, and we're very happy to have Patrice Bender from the Cap compiler team take us through the beautiful detail in this show and tell style series.

00;05;53;17 - 00;06;21;10
DJ Adams
As always, driven by curiosity and the desire to understand. By the way, there's also a related blog post just published that explains some of the amazing detail that Patrice revealed in showing how constraints are realized by pushing down to the database. This week sees the third livestream episode in this series. You can join live at the usual hands on SAP Dev time.

00;06;21;10 - 00;06;29;09
DJ Adams
It's Friday mornings in Europe, and of course you can always watch the replays at your leisure links in the description.

entry.title: Mass change of Routing Counter
entry.content: We have a case where we have 1700+ material numbers assigned to a group/counter ex: 1/20 which was mistakenly deleted at the header overview level. With that being said, A new group and counter was created in which we'd like to mass reassign all material from 1/20 into the new routing ex: 1/60. Tried CEWB and CA85N with no luck. What and how is the best transaction and best way to mass change all material in a counter to a new counter? Thanks in advance.

entry.title: SAP DMC Production Order split
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I would appreciate guidance on how to handle the following scenario using SAP Digital Manufacturing.

A production order is created in SAP S/4HANA for a total quantity of 1,000 units. On the shop floor, it is decided that production will be executed in two phases: 500 units will be produced and confirmed in the current month, and the remaining 500 units will be produced and confirmed in the following month. The requirement is that activity confirmations and related costs are reflected correctly for each respective month. Now what i want to do is that the operator first confirms the yield for example lets say 500 and then the activity confirmations are automatically calculated and posted for 500. Later when he does for the remaining 500 the activity confirmations are again calculated for 500 and posted.

I also need to make sure a record is kept of the batch or quantity of 500 that is passing through each operation and post it on a location so that it is traceable.

Currently, I am using the standard Order POD for end-to-end production execution.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Aziz Khan

SAP Digital Manufacturing 
@ManoelCosta @marcobarat

entry.title: Basics of Custom Filters in Fiori elements (OData V4)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Fiori elements, custom filters are used when standard filter fields are not sufficient. This is typically the case when you need to use a different control than the standard filter control, or when the entered value must be transformed before it is converted into OData filter conditions.

Custom filters can be created automatically from the Page Map. It generates a fragment, an event handler (if needed), and manifest.json configurations.

Figure: Adding a custom filter using Page Map

The generated fragment looks like the following:

<!-- FilterCustomer.fragment.xml -->
<ComboBox
    id="customer"
    core:require="{handler: 'ns/orders/ext/fragment/FilterCustomer'}"
    selectedKey="{path: 'filterValues>',
        type: 'sap.fe.macros.filter.type.Value',
        formatOptions: { operator: 'ns.orders.ext.fragment.FilterCustomer.filterItems' }}"
>
    <items>
        <core:Item key="0" text="Item1"/>
        <core:Item key="1" text="Item2"/>
        <core:Item key="2" text="Item3"/>
    </items>
</ComboBox>

At this point, you might wonder: What is filterValues? Or why is the value handled via a formatter?

In fact, this automatically generated pattern is not the only way to implement custom filters. There are several approaches to building custom filters, depending on your requirements.

Although the official documentation explains these options in great detail, I personally found it difficult to grasp the overall picture.
In this blog post, therefore, I will start from the absolute basics and gradually explain how custom filters work, focusing on the minimum concepts you need to understand.

All examples used in this blog post are available in the following repository:
https://github.com/miyasuta/fiori-custom-filter-blog/tree/main/app/orders/webapp

The examples were implemented and tested using SAPUI5 version 1.143.2.

 

How Custom Filters Work: The Basics You Should Know

In Fiori elements, the input of a custom filter is bound to an internal model called filterValues. This model is used by Fiori elements to support features such as variant management and app state handling for custom filters. By default, the values stored in filterValues are automatically converted into OData filter conditions, based on the configuration defined in the manifest.json file. 

In the following sections, I will introduce the common implementation patterns for custom filters.

 

Figure: Flow of custom filter values in Fiori elements

 

When No Custom Logic Is Required

When the values entered in a custom filter can be used directly for filtering without any additional processing, no event handler is required. This applies both to single-value inputs and to multi-value inputs.

In this case, you only need to bind the value of the control to the filterValues model. Fiori elements automatically generates the corresponding OData filter based on this value.

Example: Static Filter Without Custom Logic

<!-- Single Value -->
<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core="sap.ui.core" xmlns="sap.m">
        <ComboBox id="customer"
            selectedKey="{path: 'filterValues>'}"
            items="{path: 'partnerService>/Partners',
                parameters: {$filter: 'type eq \'Customer\''}}">
        <items>
            <core:Item key="{partnerService>ID}" text="{partnerService>ID}-{partnerService>name}"/>
        </items>
        </ComboBox>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 <!-- Multiple Values -->
<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core="sap.ui.core" xmlns="sap.m">
        <MultiComboBox
            id="customer2"
            selectedKeys="{path: 'filterValues>'}"
            width="100%"
            items="{path: 'partnerService>/Partners', 
                parameters: {$filter: 'type eq \'Customer\''}}"
            >
            <items>
                <core:Item key="{partnerService>ID}" text="{partnerService>ID}-{partnerService>name}"/>
            </items>
        </MultiComboBox>        
</core:FragmentDefinition>

If an operator other than EQ is required, it can be specified directly in the XML fragment using formatOptions.

selectedKey="{path: 'filterValues>', formatOptions: { operator: 'GT' }}"

You might wonder, “Where is the mapping to the OData property defined?”

This mapping is defined in the SelectionFields configuration in the manifest.json file.
Specifically, the key of filterFields ("customer" in this case) determines which OData property the filter is associated with.

Somewhat surprisingly, the property attribute itself does not play any role here.
In the following example, the value of property is changed to "banana", yet the filter continues to work as expected.

"@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionFields": {
  "filterFields": {
    "customer": {
      "label": "Customer",
      "property": "banana",
      "template": "ns.orders.ext.fragment.FilterCustomer",
      "required": false
    }
  }
}

 

When Custom Logic Is Required

In some cases, the entered value cannot be applied directly as simple filter conditions. The input may need to be mapped to a single filter condition (1:1) with custom logic, or expanded into multiple filter conditions (1:n) .

There are two main approaches to implementing such logic, which I will explain in the following sections.

Option 1: Using a Formatter-Based Operator

This approach is used in situations such as the following:

  • You want to apply filters to arbitrary properties, regardless of the filterFields configuration defined in the manifest.json

  • You need filter logic that is more complex than a simple EQ condition, for example when multiple conditions need to be combined

The mechanism works as follows: by specifying the binding type sap.fe.macros.filter.type.Value or sap.fe.macros.filter.type.MultiValue, a custom operator is triggered during filter generation. The custom operator receives the input values and converts them into one or more Filter objects (sap.ui.model.Filter). The resulting filters are then applied to the OData query.

 

Figure: Flow of formatter-based operator converting input values to Filter objects

Key limitation: A custom operator can only access the values passed through the binding and cannot access other properties of the control or the Extension API.
Therefore, this option is suitable when the filter logic can be expressed solely based on the values provided via the binding.

Example: Custom Operator with Single Input

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core="sap.ui.core" xmlns="sap.m">
        <ComboBox
            id="supplier"
            core:require="{handler: 'ns/orders/ext/fragment/SingleInputWithFormatterOperator'}"
            selectedKey="{path: 'filterValues>', type: 'sap.fe.macros.filter.type.Value', formatOptions: { operator: 'ns.orders.ext.fragment.SingleInputWithFormatterOperator.filterItems' }}"
            items="{path: 'partnerService>/Partners'}">
        <items>
            <core:Item key="{partnerService>ID}" text="{partnerService>ID}-{partnerService>name}"/>
        </items>
        </ComboBox>
</core:FragmentDefinition>
export function filterItems(value: string) {
    const inputSupplier = parseInt(value, 10);
    return new Filter({ path: "supplier", operator: FilterOperator.EQ, value1: inputSupplier });

    // Alternatively, you can return multiple filters
    // return new Filter({
    //     filters: [
    //         new Filter({ path: "supplier", operator: FilterOperator.GE, value1: 2 }),
    //         new Filter({ path: "supplier", operator: FilterOperator.LE, value1: 4 })
    //     ],
    //     and: true
    // });
}

Example: Custom Operator with Multiple Inputs

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core="sap.ui.core" xmlns="sap.m">
        <MultiComboBox
            id="supplier2"
            core:require="{handler: 'ns/orders/ext/fragment/MultiInputWithFormetterOperator'}"
            selectedKeys="{path: 'filterValues>', type: 'sap.fe.macros.filter.type.MultiValue', formatOptions: { operator: 'ns.orders.ext.fragment.MultiInputWithFormetterOperator.filterItems' }}"
            items="{path: 'partnerService>/Partners', 
                parameters: {$filter: 'type eq \'Supplier\''}}"
            >
            <items>
                <core:Item key="{partnerService>ID}" text="{partnerService>ID}-{partnerService>name}"/>
            </items>
        </MultiComboBox>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

In the case of multiple inputs, the custom operator receives an array of values. You need to iterate over this array and convert each value into the corresponding Filter objects.

export function filterItems(values: string[]) {
    const filters = values.map((value) => {
        const inputSupplier = parseInt(value, 10);
        return new Filter({ path: "supplier2", operator: FilterOperator.EQ, value1: inputSupplier });
    });

    return new Filter({
        filters: filters,
        and: false
    });
}

 

Option 2: Using an Event Handler and the Extension API

This approach is used in situations such as the following:

  • When you need to access additional properties of a control and use them for filtering, for example using the text value of a ComboBox instead of only the selected key

  • When you need to perform additional processing, such as calling an OData service, to convert the input value into the appropriate filter values

In this case, the filter logic is implemented inside a control event handler. The filters are then set explicitly by calling the Extension API method setFilterValues().

 

Figure: Flow of event handler approach using setFilterValues() via Extension API

Key limitation: The setFilterValues() method can only be used for properties of the main entity of the List Report, for keys defined in the filterFields section of the manifest.json, or for properties of associated entities specified in navigationProperties. The next section explains how to handle this limitation with sample code.

Example: Setting Filters with Event Handler

In the following example, an event handler is assigned to the change event of a ComboBox.

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns:core="sap.ui.core" xmlns="sap.m">
        <ComboBox
            id="contact"
            core:require="{handler: 'ns/orders/ext/fragment/SingleInputWithEventHandler'}"
            selectedKey="{path: 'filterValues>'}"
            width="100%"
            items="{path: 'partnerService>/Partners', 
                parameters: {$filter: 'type eq \'Contact\''}}"
            change='handler.onContactChange'>
        <items>
            <core:Item key="{partnerService>ID}" text="{partnerService>ID}-{partnerService>name}"/>
        </items>
        </ComboBox>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

The following is a simple example of controller logic. The value set in the control is applied to the filter by calling setFilterValues(). If no value is provided, no additional handling is required. The Fiori elements framework automatically excludes the filter in this case.

export function onContactChange(this: ExtensionAPI, oEvent: ComboBox$ChangeEvent) {
    const selectedKey = oEvent.getSource().getSelectedKey();

    if (selectedKey) {
        this.setFilterValues("contact", selectedKey);
        // Alternatively, you can use a filter operator
        //this.setFilterValues("contact", 'GT', selectedKey);
    }
}

 

Specifying Properties of Navigation Targets in an Event Handler

When specifying properties of navigation targets using the setFilterValues() method, there are several important points to consider:

  • The keys in the filterFields section of the manifest.json must be written in the <navigationProperty>/<field> format.
    Even for key fields, you must specify them in this format, for example as contact/ID instead of contact_ID.

  • When setting filters for multiple properties of navigation targets, any properties that are not defined as keys in filterFields must be specified in navigationProperties.

Example

In the following example, two properties of the navigation target, contact2/ID and contact2/firstName, are set as filters.

The key contact2/ID is defined in the filterFields section. The property contact2/firstName is specified in navigationProperties.

"@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionFields": {
  "navigationProperties": [
    "contact2/firstName"
  ],
  "filterFields": {
    "contact2/ID": {
      "label": "Contact2 (NavigationPropertyWithEventHandler)",
      "property": "contact2/ID",
      "template": "ns.orders.ext.fragment.NavigationPropertyWithEventHandler",
      "required": false
    }
  }
}

In the event handler, these two properties are explicitly set as filters.
Please note that the value for firstName is hard-coded in this example solely to demonstrate how multiple navigation properties can be set.

export function onContactChange(this: ExtensionAPI, oEvent: ComboBox$ChangeEvent) {
    const selectedKey = oEvent.getSource().getSelectedKey();

    if (selectedKey) {
        this.setFilterValues("contact2/ID", selectedKey);
        this.setFilterValues("contact2/firstName", "Taro");
    }
}

 

Conclusion: Formatter-Based Operator or Event Handler?

In most cases, the recommended approach is to use a formatter-based operator. This option allows you to define filters without being restricted by the configuration specified in manifest.json, and it also supports more complex filter logic, such as combining multiple conditions using OR.

A custom event handler should be used only when access to additional control properties, external APIs, or the Extension API is required. In such scenarios, the event handler approach provides the necessary flexibility.

To summarize the decision process, the following diagram shows how to choose the appropriate implementation approach:

Decision tree for selecting the appropriate custom filter implementation approach

Use this flow as a quick guideline when deciding which pattern to adopt in your own applications.

entry.title: Re: Default bin location with DTW
entry.content:
Hi everyon,

I've extracted the Item details using the @Amir_Tamboli query.
However I got the error "Data Transfer workbench error: Index was outside the bounds of the array: File: C:\... check for missing columns" when I tried to create the CSV only with ItemCode, LineNum and BinLocation.

So, I created a new CSV with all OITW columns in DTW > Customize template;
And populated onlyt  the Item Code, LineNum, Warehouse and Default Bin Location (AbsEntry).
It worked fine.

entry.title: Re: Mass deletion of Bankdetails in BUT0BK
entry.content:
As far as I know, bank details cannot be changed in bulk. You might be able to use LSMW, but this process would be rather difficult on the BP screen. The most ideal method would be to use the function module BAPI_BUPA_BANKDETAIL_CHANGE. In this function, you enter the BP number in the BUSINESSPARTNER field and the bank identifier in BANKDETAILID, and then you can change the field you want in the BANKDETAILDATA section. In the BANKDETAILDATA_X section, you should put an “X” for the field you want to change.

You can achieve a mass update by developing an ABAP program with the help of an ABAP consultant, and you can even use an Excel upload template for the data transfer.

Additionally, you can access existing bank details using BAPI_BUPA_BANKDETAILS_GET, add new bank details with BAPI_BUPA_BANKDETAIL_ADD, and delete bank details using BAPI_BUPA_BANKDETAIL_REMOVE.

entry.title: PR/PO deletion Flexible Workflow issue
entry.content:
Currently, the workflow is not triggered when a PR line item is deleted with flexible workflow in place. Additionally, if all PO line items are deleted, the workflow is not re-triggered with flexible workflow in place


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: PR/PO deletion Flexible Workflow issue
entry.content:
Hi, 

If the PR and PO are In Approval status, then the Restart BADi may suit your business need: 

When a purchase requisition line item is deleted in a fully approved PR, the system updates EBAN directly and does not call the workflow restart logic in class CL_MM_PUR_APPROVAL.

Because of this design, the BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PR_WFL_RESTART is not triggered for item deletion, even though it is triggered for other changes or when the PR is not fully approved.

Since item deletion is intentionally excluded from workflow-relevant change evaluation, the workflow will not restart in this scenario. 

For more information on the Restart BADI for PR, please review here: MM_PUR_S4_PR_WFL_RESTART
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/af9ef57f504840d2b81be8667206d485/91ab576eaf58475f8934d2dcef16edb3-619.html?locale=en-US&version=2023.latest

Also for PO's In Approval, when all items are deleted as per standard the restart is not started as per standard however the BADI can influence this. Again for more information on BADI MMPUR_PO_WORKFLOW_RESTART, please review here: 
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/af9ef57f504840d2b81be8667206d485/c1acc9fa4c2041be952bb7c1c8f902ae.html?locale=en-US&version=2023.latest

Kind Regards 
Lisa 
SAP Support

entry.title: Meet Joule: Your AI Sidekick / AI Co-pilot Inside SAP
entry.content:
Meet Joule: Your AI Sidekick / AI Co-pilot Inside SAP

Tired of wrestling with complex SAP systems? Say hello to Joule, SAP's new AI copilot designed to make your work life easier and more efficient. Think of Joule as your intelligent assistant, ready to help you navigate SAP, get instant answers, and achieve better outcomes – all by simply asking questions in plain English.

What Makes Joule Special?

Joule isn't just another chatbot. It's a powerful AI engine that understands your business context and can orchestrate actions across various SAP systems. Here's what Joule brings to the table:

  • One-Stop Shop: Joule acts as a single front-end, providing a consistent user experience across all SAP cloud products, whether you're on your desktop or mobile device.
  • Increased Efficiency: SAP claims Joule can make employees up to 80% more efficient by helping them easily find information, execute transactions, and gain analytical insights.
  • Orchestrating AI Agents: Joule can coordinate multiple AI agents working in different areas like finance, HR, and supply chain to solve complex problems. Imagine a sustainability agent recommending ways to reduce your carbon footprint, pulling data from various departments!
  • Context-Aware Intelligence: Joule provides responses tailored to your specific role, the SAP transaction you're working on, and your company's unique business data.
  • Security First: Joule inherits your existing user authorization and authentication rights, ensuring that sensitive data is protected.

 What Can Joule Do for You? What are the Joule Interaction Patterns?

  • Informational: Joule provides quick and easy access to information within SAP systems. Users can ask questions in natural language to retrieve data, definitions, or explanations related to business processes, master data, or system configurations.
    • Examples:
      • "What is the current stock level of material X?"
      • "Explain the process for creating a purchase order."
      • "What are the payment terms for vendor Y?"
  • Navigational: Joule simplifies navigation within SAP, guiding users to the correct screens, reports, or functions to accomplish their tasks. It eliminates the need to memorize complex SAP menus or transaction codes.
    • Examples:
      • "Show me the sales order overview."
      • "Take me to the screen for creating a new customer."
      • "Where can I find the report on overdue invoices?"
  • Transactional: Joule enables users to execute transactions directly through the copilot interface, streamlining processes like creating orders, updating records, or approving requests. This reduces the need to manually navigate through multiple screens and fields.
    • Examples:
      • "Create a sales order for customer Z with 10 units of product A."
      • "Approve the purchase requisition for laptop computers."
      • "Change the address for employee ID 12345."
  • Analytical: Joule provides analytical insights and visualizations based on SAP data. Users can request reports, dashboards, or predictive analyses to identify trends, monitor performance, and make data-driven decisions.
    • Examples:
      • "Show me a sales report for the last quarter."
      • "What is the projected cash flow for the next month?"
      • "Identify the top 10 performing products by revenue.

Joule Gets Smarter All the Time:

SAP is continuously expanding Joule's capabilities. It already covers a significant portion of the most-used transactions in SAP and offers skills tailored to various business roles. Plus, Joule is learning to personalize content based on your preferences and can connect to data in third-party systems.

Better Together: Joule and Microsoft Copilot

Recognizing that users don't want to juggle multiple copilots, SAP and Microsoft are integrating Joule with Microsoft Copilot for Office 365. This means you can leverage both copilots seamlessly, no matter where you are working.

Consultant's Best Friend:

SAP is even introducing SAP Consulting capabilities within Joule, trained on a vast database of SAP knowledge. This can save consultants hours each day, accelerating cloud migrations and business transformations.

The Bottom Line:

Joule promises to be a gamechanger for SAP users, offering a more intuitive and efficient way to work. By harnessing the power of AI, Joule empowers users to get more done in less time, ultimately driving better business outcomes.
